quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Index of refraction"?

options.text: 1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ + ...; total voltage = sum of individual voltages; same charge on each capacitor
Intensity I ∝ A²; for spherical waves: I = P/(4πr²), intensity decreases as 1/r²
n = c/v; the ratio of light speed in vacuum to light speed in the medium; n > 1 for all materials (n = 1.0 for vacuum, 1.33 for water, 1.5 for glass)
J.J. Thomson used crossed E and B fields to measure the electron's charge-to-mass ratio e/m, discovering the electron

quiz_questions.explanation: "Index of refraction" is defined as: n = c/v; the ratio of light speed in vacuum to light speed in the medium; n > 1 for all materials (n = 1.0 for vacuum, 1.33 for water, 1.5 for glass). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Snell's law"?

options.text: Two converging lenses (objective + eyepiece); total magnification M = m_objective × M_eyepiece; enlarges nearby small objects
d_o > 0 (real object); d_i > 0 (real image, opposite side); d_i  0 (converging); f < 0 (diverging)
n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂; relates angles of incidence and refraction to the indices of refraction of two media
Angular momentum L = nℏ (n = 1,2,3,...); equivalently, nλ = 2πr (standing wave condition for electron orbit)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Snell's law" is defined as: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂; relates angles of incidence and refraction to the indices of refraction of two media. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Total internal reflection"?

options.text: Occurs when light travels from high-n to low-n medium at θ > θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); all light is reflected, none transmitted
When two waves meet, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference patterns
F = kq₁q₂/r²; k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²; inversely proportional to r²; analogous to gravitational force
V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt); alternates between +V₀ and -V₀; ω = 2πf; US standard: 120V RMS at 60 Hz

quiz_questions.explanation: "Total internal reflection" is defined as: Occurs when light travels from high-n to low-n medium at θ > θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); all light is reflected, none transmitted. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Dispersion"?

options.text: Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows
τ = nIAB sin θ; n = number of turns, I = current, A = loop area, B = field strength, θ = angle between area vector and B
F_b = ρ_fluid · V_displaced · g; an object floats when its weight equals the buoyant force (average density < fluid density)
Crossed E and B fields: only particles with v = E/B pass straight through; magnetic and electric forces cancel exactly

quiz_questions.explanation: "Dispersion" is defined as: Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Apparent depth"?

options.text: Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)
Node at closed end, antinode at open: f_n = nv/(4L), n = 1,3,5,... (odd harmonics only); e.g., clarinet, stopped pipe
B = μ₀I/(2πr); circular field lines around a long straight wire; strength decreases with distance r from wire
Strong attraction and permanent magnetization; magnetic domains align cooperatively; responsible for permanent magnets

quiz_questions.explanation: "Apparent depth" is defined as: Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "n = c/v; the ratio of light speed in vacuum to light speed in the medium; n > 1 for all materials (n = 1.0 for vacuum, 1.33 for water, 1.5 for glass)"?

options.text: Wave energy
Electric field
Root-mean-square speed
Index of refraction

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Index of refraction".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂; relates angles of incidence and refraction to the indices of refraction of two media"?

options.text: Bernoulli's equation
Ohm's law
Convex mirror
Snell's law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Snell's law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Occurs when light travels from high-n to low-n medium at θ > θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); all light is reflected, none transmitted"?

options.text: Total internal reflection
Magnetic field of a wire
Absorption spectrum
Sound intensity level

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Total internal reflection".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Different wavelengths refract by different amounts (n varies with λ); produces spectrum separation in prisms and rainbows"?

options.text: Right-hand rule (force)
Dispersion
Convex mirror
Brewster's angle

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Dispersion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)"?

options.text: Pauli exclusion principle
Apparent depth
Root-mean-square speed
Heisenberg uncertainty principle

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Apparent depth".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Refraction and Snell's Law is accurate?

options.text: Entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics governs the direction of natural processes.
Electrons and Atomic Models traces the evolution of atomic theory from Thomson's plum pudding model to Bohr's quantized orbits to the quantum mechanical model.
Refraction and Snell's Law describes how light bends when crossing boundaries between media of different optical densities.
Ideal Gas Behavior is described by the ideal gas law: PV = nRT (or PV = NkT), relating pressure P, volume V, temperature T, and amount of gas n (moles) or N (molecules).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Refraction and Snell's Law as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Magnetic Properties of Materials classifies materials by their response to external magnetic fields.
Snell's law: n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂, where n is the index of refraction (n = c/v).
The law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection (θᵢ = θᵣ), measured from the normal.
EMF can be induced by changing B, changing A, or changing the angle θ between B and the area vector.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Refraction and Snell's Law as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Light bends toward the normal when entering a denser medium (higher n) and away when entering a less dense medium.
Wave Properties and Behavior explores the fundamental characteristics common to all waves.
Ideal Gas Behavior is described by the ideal gas law: PV = nRT (or PV = NkT), relating pressure P, volume V, temperature T, and amount of gas n (moles) or N (molecules).
In hydrogen: E_n = -13.6 eV/n².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Refraction and Snell's Law as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Plane mirrors produce virtual, upright, same-size images located behind the mirror.
Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio: light stable nuclei have N ≈ Z; heavier nuclei need more neutrons (N > Z).
Wave Properties and Behavior explores the fundamental characteristics common to all waves.
Total internal reflection occurs when light hits the boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle: θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁), and only when going from high-n to low-n medium.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Refraction and Snell's Law as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Electromagnetic waves consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation.
Refraction and Snell's Law describes how light bends when crossing boundaries between media of different optical densities.
The electromagnetic force governs the behavior of all charged matter, from electrons in atoms to current in wires.
A parallel-plate capacitor has capacitance C = ε₀A/d (area A, plate separation d).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Refraction and Snell's Law as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: CRT televisions and oscilloscopes: electron beams are steered by electric and magnetic fields to paint images on a phosphor screen
Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure
Fiber optics: light pulses travel through thin glass fibers via total internal reflection, carrying internet data at near-light speed over thousands of kilometers
Carbon dating: the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic material decreases over time due to ¹⁴C decay; measuring this ratio determines the age of archaeological artifacts

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations
Wireless charging (Qi standard): a changing magnetic field in the charging pad induces an EMF in the receiving coil inside the phone
Rainbows: sunlight refracts upon entering water droplets, reflects internally, and refracts again upon exiting—dispersion separates colors at different angles
Flash memory (USB drives, SSDs) stores data using quantum tunneling: electrons tunnel through a thin oxide layer to charge a floating gate, representing binary data

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Refraction and Snell's Law applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Atomic clocks use the precise frequency of electron transitions in cesium atoms (9.19 GHz) to define the second—accurate to 1 part in 10¹⁴
Eyeglasses and contact lenses correct vision by refracting light to focus images correctly on the retina, compensating for the eye's refractive errors
Lightning: charge separation in thunderclouds creates enormous electric fields (≈3 × 10⁶ V/m) that ionize air and create conductive channels, equalizing charge via massive current discharge
Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Refraction and Snell's Law?

options.text: Fiber optics: light pulses travel through thin glass fibers via total internal reflection, carrying internet data at near-light speed over thousands of kilometers
Defibrillators deliver a large potential difference (~1000-3000 V) across the heart to reset its electrical rhythm during cardiac arrest
Scanning tunneling microscopes use quantum tunneling current between a sharp tip and a surface to image individual atoms on surfaces with sub-atomic resolution
Thermonuclear weapons use fission to trigger fusion: the fission bomb creates temperatures and pressures needed to fuse hydrogen isotopes, releasing vastly more energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Refraction and Snell's Law in practice?

options.text: Phone chargers contain transformers (and rectifiers) that convert 120V AC to 5-20V DC for safe device charging
Rainbows: sunlight refracts upon entering water droplets, reflects internally, and refracts again upon exiting—dispersion separates colors at different angles
Arrow of time: entropy increase explains why ice melts in warm rooms but warm rooms don't spontaneously freeze—the second law governs the direction of all natural processes
Radio telescope arrays (like the VLA) combine signals from many dishes using interference principles to achieve resolution equivalent to a single dish spanning kilometers

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Refraction and Snell's Law.
